mcqs.question: What is thrashing in virtual memory systems?

options.text: A normal paging operation when pages are swapped between memory and disk
A state of excessive paging activity causing severe performance degradation
A method for allocating frames to processes
A page replacement algorithm

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing is the pathological situation where the CPU spends most of its time servicing page faults rather than executing instructions, due to excessive paging activity from insufficient memory.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a primary cause of thrashing?

options.text: Too few processes in memory (underloading)
Insufficient frames allocated to processes leading to high page fault rate
Fast CPU processing speed
Large disk cache size

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when processes do not have enough memory frames to hold their working sets, causing them to constantly page fault and page back in.

mcqs.question: In the thrashing curve, what happens to CPU utilization as degree of multiprogramming increases beyond the thrashing point?

options.text: CPU utilization continues to increase linearly
CPU utilization remains constant
CPU utilization decreases sharply due to thrashing
CPU utilization becomes 100%

mcqs.explanation: Beyond the optimal point, adding more processes causes thrashing where CPU utilization drops sharply because most time is spent on page I/O rather than computation.

mcqs.question: The Working Set Model for controlling thrashing is based on which concept?

options.text: The total number of pages in physical memory
The set of pages a process has referenced in the last τ time units
The page fault rate threshold
The number of free frames in the system

mcqs.explanation: The working set of a process is the set of pages referenced in the recent past (defined by the window size τ). The system must allocate enough frames to accommodate each process's working set.

mcqs.question: Which technique directly monitors page fault rate to control thrashing?

options.text: Working Set Model
Page Fault Frequency (PFF) control
LRU replacement algorithm
Global page replacement

mcqs.explanation: PFF directly monitors each process's page fault frequency. If the rate exceeds an upper threshold, more frames are allocated; if below a lower threshold, frames are reclaimed.

mcqs.question: In which state does a process spend most of its time during thrashing?

options.text: Running state
Ready state waiting for CPU
Blocked state waiting for page I/O
Terminated state

mcqs.explanation: During thrashing, processes are frequently blocked waiting for page faults to be serviced, meaning they are in blocked state waiting for page I/O operations.

mcqs.question: What is the key difference between local and global page replacement in relation to thrashing?

options.text: Local replacement is faster than global replacement
Local replacement prevents a process from taking frames from another process, helping contain thrashing
Global replacement always prevents thrashing
There is no difference

mcqs.explanation: Local replacement confines each process to its own set of frames, protecting other processes from its page faults. Global replacement can cause one thrashing process to steal frames from others, accelerating system-wide thrashing.

mcqs.question: A system has 100 frames and is running multiple processes. If each process needs at least 10 frames for its working set, what is the maximum number of processes that can run without thrashing?
mcqs.explanation: With 100 frames total and each process needing minimum 10 frames for its working set, maximum processes = 100/10 = 10. Beyond this, processes will not have enough frames and thrashing may occur.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a symptom of thrashing?

options.text: High CPU utilization close to 100%
Extremely high page fault rate
Heavy disk I/O activity
Processes spending time in blocked state

mcqs.explanation: During thrashing, CPU utilization is very LOW because the CPU is idle waiting for page I/O operations. High CPU utilization indicates the system is working efficiently, not thrashing.

mcqs.question: What happens when the idle process becomes very active in a thrashing system?

options.text: The system is working efficiently with good CPU utilization
It indicates processes are not getting enough memory frames and are being swapped
The system has too much memory allocated to processes
Page faults have completely stopped

mcqs.explanation: The idle process runs when there are no ready processes. If it's very active (swapping), it means processes are constantly blocked waiting for pages, indicating thrashing.

mcqs.question: The LRU (Least Recently Used) page replacement algorithm helps reduce thrashing because it:

options.text: Always keeps the most recently used pages in memory
Evicts pages that have not been used recently, approximating the optimal replacement
Gives priority to lower priority processes
Uses a fixed number of frames per process

mcqs.explanation: LRU approximates optimal page replacement by evicting pages that have not been used in the longest time, which is more likely to be pages not in the current working set.

mcqs.question: What is the main goal of thrashing control strategies?

options.text: To eliminate page faults completely
To maintain a balance between CPU utilization and degree of multiprogramming while keeping page fault rate acceptable
To maximize the number of processes in memory
To use global replacement only

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing control aims to find the optimal operating point where CPU utilization is high while keeping the page fault rate at a manageable level.

mcqs.question: If a system is thrashing, which of the following actions would be MOST appropriate to reduce thrashing?

options.text: Increase degree of multiprogramming by adding more processes
Temporarily suspend or swap out some processes to reduce memory demand
Install a slower disk to reduce I/O speed
Disable virtual memory completely

mcqs.explanation: Suspending or swapping out some processes reduces the demand for frames, allowing remaining processes to have enough memory for their working sets, reducing thrashing.

mcqs.question: The 'thrashing curve' illustrates the relationship between:

options.text: Page fault rate and CPU speed
Degree of multiprogramming and CPU utilization
Number of frames and page size
Disk I/O time and memory size

mcqs.explanation: The thrashing curve plots CPU utilization against degree of multiprogramming, showing how utilization increases to a peak then drops sharply as thrashing begins.

mcqs.question: Priority-based page replacement for thrashing control gives preferential treatment to:

options.text: All processes equally
Lower priority processes
Higher priority processes when allocating frames
Processes with more page faults

mcqs.explanation: Priority-based replacement ensures higher priority processes get the frames they need, potentially allowing lower priority processes to lose frames and thrash, protecting critical system processes.

mcqs.question: Which statement about copy-on-write and thrashing is correct?

options.text: Copy-on-write eliminates all page faults and prevents thrashing
Copy-on-write can reduce unnecessary page duplication and help manage memory more efficiently, potentially reducing thrashing
Copy-on-write causes more thrashing than regular paging
Copy-on-write has no relation to thrashing

mcqs.explanation: Copy-on-write delays page duplication until a write occurs, saving memory. This more efficient use of memory can reduce the chance of thrashing.

mcqs.question: In demand paging, when is a page brought into memory?

options.text: Before it is referenced (pre-paging)
Only when a page fault occurs and the page is referenced
At system startup only
Never - pages are always in memory

mcqs.explanation: Demand paging only loads pages when a page fault occurs, meaning the page is needed but not in memory. This can lead to thrashing if processes don't have enough frames.

mcqs.question: What is the effect of increasing the frame allocation to a process that is thrashing?

options.text: The process will continue to thrash at the same rate
It may reduce or eliminate thrashing for that process by allowing it to hold its working set
It will cause more processes to thrash
It has no effect on thrashing

mcqs.explanation: If a thrashing process receives more frames, it can hold more of its working set in memory, reducing page faults and potentially eliminating its thrashing condition.

mcqs.question: The optimal degree of multiprogramming in a virtual memory system is:

options.text: Always maximum possible number of processes
The point just before thrashing begins where CPU utilization is highest
Always minimum number of processes
Equal to the number of CPU cores

mcqs.explanation: The optimal degree is the point just before the thrashing zone where CPU utilization is maximized. Adding more processes beyond this point causes thrashing and reduces performance.

mcqs.question: Pre-paging attempts to prevent thrashing by:

options.text: Loading pages only when they are faulted
Bringing pages into memory before they are needed based on predicted reference patterns
Using global page replacement only
Never loading any pages

mcqs.explanation: Pre-paging tries to anticipate which pages will be needed and loads them before a fault occurs. When successful, it reduces page faults and helps prevent thrashing.
